title: Nonreciprocity-enriched steady phases in open quantum systems
authors: Ding Gu, Zhanpeng Fu, Zhong Wang
abstract: Nonreciprocity can profoundly alter the spectra and dynamics of open quantum systems, yet its impact on the long-time steady-state phases of matter has remained largely unexplored. Here we show that the interplay of nonreciprocity, symmetry defects, and spatial boundaries can generate phases beyond the standard spontaneous-symmetry-breaking paradigm. We demonstrate this mechanism by showing that sufficiently strong nonreciprocity turns boundaries into sources and drains of symmetry defects, while simultaneously endowing these defects with chiral dynamics in the bulk. As a result, the conventional uniform symmetry-broken state gives way to a domain-wall traveling-wave phase, in which symmetry defects form a persistent chiral wave. We showcase this mechanism in a bosonic model with \(Z_{2}\) symmetry, where periodic boundary conditions support only the conventional symmetric and symmetry-broken phases, whereas open boundary conditions allow the traveling-wave phase. We further show that even in the absence of symmetry breaking, the steady state can exhibit anomalous chiral relaxation: owing to the non-Hermitian skin effect in the stability matrix, local fluctuations are chirally amplified as they approach a boundary, where they eventually decay. Combining mean-field theory with truncated Wigner simulations, we characterize these phases, analyze the order parameter and Goldstone-mode fluctuations of the traveling-wave phase, and confirm its existence in three spatial dimensions.
summary: This paper demonstrates that nonreciprocity can create entirely new steady-state phases in open quantum systems, such as a traveling-wave phase of symmetry defects. It shows that while periodic systems exhibit standard symmetry breaking, open boundaries allow for chiral dynamics driven by the non-Hermitian skin effect. This work expands the understanding of how non-Hermitian physics can fundamentally alter the phase diagram of driven-dissipative matter.
main_problem: Investigating how nonreciprocity and the non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE) can stabilize new non-equilibrium steady-state phases of matter beyond the standard spontaneous-symmetry-breaking paradigm.
main_result: The discovery of a boundary-induced 'traveling-wave phase' where nonreciprocity drives domain walls to form a persistent chiral wave, as well as a 'chiral damping phase' where local fluctuations are amplified by the NHSE.
method: The study employs mean-field theory, linear stability analysis, and truncated Wigner simulations to characterize phase boundaries and fluctuation dynamics.
model_or_system: A driven-dissipative bosonic model with Z2 symmetry, featuring a Lindblad master equation with nonreciprocal hopping (iJ), single-particle gain, and two-particle loss.
key_observables: Order parameter (expectation value of the bosonic field), density, and the autocorrelation function for coherence time.
important_parameters: Nonreciprocity strength (J), gain/loss parameters (kappa1, kappa2), and spatial boundary conditions (PBC vs OBC).
main_assumptions: The continuum limit approximation neglects spatial variations of the time-derivative of the mean-field solution near domain walls; the Wigner method relies on truncating third-order derivative terms.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the model lattice and representative examples of the three OBC phases (uniform, traveling-wave, and damping) alongside the effective Hamiltonian spectrum. Figure S1 shows the time evolution and exponential decay of the autocorrelation function amplitude.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the model and the problem of nonreciprocity in steady states, presents the discovery of the traveling-wave and chiral damping phases under OBC, analyzes the role of the non-Hermitian skin effect, and concludes with a stability analysis of Goldstone-mode fluctuations using numerical simulations.
why_interesting: It provides a new mechanism for phase formation where spatial boundaries and nonreciprocity interact to create phases that are topologically or dynamically distinct from their periodic counterparts, directly impacting the study of non-equilibrium many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. Phase diagram of the model with OBC along x, PBC along y, z. (a) Phase diagram. As the one-body gain κ1 increases, the system passes from the chiral damping phase to the traveling wave phase, and then to the uniform phase. Data are averaged over the y and z directions, since the observables are uniform along these directions. FIG. 3. System-size scaling of the traveling wave coherence time. For the system sizes accessible with our present numer- ical resources, τ0 increases strongly with Ly and Lz, following τ0 ∼L2.147, while remaining nearly independent of Lx. Pa- rameters are Kx = 0.8, κ1 = 1.5, Ky = Kz = 4.0, J = 3.0, λ = 1.0, and κ2 = 0.2.

FIG. 4. Order parameter under PBC along the x direction. The steady state remains uniform as κ1 is varied. Red dots denote numerical results, while the green line shows the mean- field prediction. Inset: time evolution of the order parameter, illustrating rapid relaxation to the steady state. The initial condition is αr,0 = 0.6−0.6i. Parameters are J = 3.0, λ = 1.0, Kx = 0.8, Ky = Kz = 4.0, and κ2 = 0.2, with Lx = Ly = Lz = 10.
